findings.text: order-service DB connection pool is fully saturated with active connections at the configured maximum, causing connection wait timeouts and checkout failures that correlate with the observed error spikes
CPU utilization for order-service has been sustained above 90% for an extended period, indicating the service is operating well beyond its designed capacity
The service is running with only 2 desired tasks, which is undersized for the current load — insufficient concurrency to absorb traffic spikes
The order-service DB client uses a fixed pool size with no adaptive backoff on checkout failure, meaning failed connection attempts pile up and amplify connection churn under load
No lock contention on primary tables — the bottleneck is purely pool sizing, not database-level contention
No recent deployments or configuration changes in the last 24 hours — this is a load-driven incident, not a change-driven one

body.potentialRootCause: The root cause is a combination of insufficient horizontal capacity and a rigid DB connection pool configuration in order-service. Under elevated load, the fixed-size connection pool (no backoff, no adaptive sizing) fills to its maximum, causing all subsequent DB checkout attempts to queue and eventually timeout. With only 2 tasks running, the service lacks the concurrency headroom to absorb traffic spikes. The sustained >90% CPU confirms the service is operating beyond its designed capacity. The result is a cascading failure: DB waiters grow, threads saturate, and the service becomes unresponsive. No recent code or config changes triggered this — it is purely a capacity and resilience issue.

recommendedActions.label: Scale order-service tasks from 2 to 6
recommendedActions.description: Increase desired task count from 2 to 6 to provide sufficient concurrency headroom for the current load. This directly addresses the undersized capacity identified by infra inspector (desired=2) and the sustained >90% CPU observed by metric analyst.
recommendedActions.rationale: With only 2 tasks running and CPU above 90%, the service cannot absorb traffic spikes. Scaling to 6 tasks provides 3x concurrency headroom and distributes the DB connection load across more instances, reducing per-instance pool pressure.

recommendedActions.label: Increase DB connection pool size for order-service
recommendedActions.description: Raise the configured maximum DB connections for order-service beyond the current saturation point. The pool is at max with active waiters growing, so the ceiling must be raised to allow new connections to be acquired.
recommendedActions.rationale: DB analyst confirmed pool max is reached with waiters growing. The fixed pool size is the direct bottleneck — increasing it allows the service to acquire connections when the pool is under pressure, breaking the deadlock cycle.

recommendedActions.label: Add idle timeout to DB connections in order-service
recommendedActions.description: Configure an idle timeout on DB connections (e.g., 300s) to prevent connection leaks. Combined with the fixed pool and no backoff, leaked connections contribute to pool saturation under load.
recommendedActions.rationale: Code analyzer found no adaptive backoff on checkout failure. Without timeouts, leaked/stale connections accumulate and prevent new connections from being acquired, worsening pool saturation.

recommendedActions.label: Open PR to add adaptive backoff on DB checkout failures
recommendedActions.description: Create a pull request that adds exponential backoff with jitter to DB connection checkout failures in order-service, replacing the current fixed-pool-without-backoff behavior.
recommendedActions.rationale: Code analyzer identified that the DB client uses a fixed pool size with no adaptive backoff on checkout failure. Adding backoff prevents connection churn amplification under load and is a structural fix for this class of incident.

recommendedActions.label: Add response cache for order-service read paths
recommendedActions.description: Introduce a caching layer (e.g., ElastiCache or application-level cache) for order-service read-heavy paths to reduce DB connection demand.
recommendedActions.rationale: With CPU sustained above 90% and pool at max, reducing DB connection demand directly alleviates the bottleneck. A cache layer decouples read traffic from the DB, buying time while the code fix is developed.

recommendedActions.label: Page on-call engineer for manual DB pool tuning
recommendedActions.description: Escalate to the on-call engineer to verify the new pool size and timeout settings are applied, and to monitor waiter count regression in real-time.
recommendedActions.rationale: Pool size and timeout changes require manual verification in the deployment pipeline. The on-call engineer needs to confirm the changes take effect and validate waiter count decreases.

evidence.content: Log analyst: order-service shows connection pool wait timeouts and thread-pool saturation under high CPU. Errors correlate with DB checkout failures. Key finding: Connection pool exhaustion under CPU pressure. Confidence: high.

Metric analyst: CPUUtilization sustained above 90% for order-service. Concurrent DB connections at pool max; queue depth rising. Key finding: Sustained high CPU with saturated DB pool. Confidence: high.
Infra inspector: order-service running with 2 desired tasks. Task definition CPU/memory appear undersized for current load. Key finding: Capacity undersized for load. Confidence: medium.

DB analyst: Connection waiters spiked while active connections hit the configured maximum. No lock contention on primary tables; pool sizing is the bottleneck. Key finding: Pool max reached, waiters growing. Confidence: high.

Code analyzer: order-service DB client uses a fixed pool size with no adaptive backoff on checkout failure. Recent code paths amplify connection churn under load. Key finding: Fixed pool size without backoff. Confidence: medium.
Change detector: No recent deployments in the last 24h. Configuration unchanged. Issue appears load-driven rather than change-driven. Key finding: No correlating deploy. Confidence: high.

customerExplanation.summary: The order-service, which handles customer orders, ran out of database connections under heavy load. Its connection pool was fully saturated with no way to acquire new connections, causing requests to queue and eventually fail. The service was also undersized (only 2 instances) for the current traffic, and had no backoff mechanism to handle connection failures gracefully.
customerExplanation.impact: Customers experienced order processing failures and timeouts as the service could not complete database operations. Queue depth rose as requests piled up waiting for DB access.
customerExplanation.resolution: Scaling the service from 2 to 6 tasks to restore capacity, increasing the DB connection pool size to allow new connections, and adding connection idle timeouts to prevent leaks. A code fix PR is being opened to add adaptive backoff on connection failures.
customerExplanation.eta: 30 minutes for immediate capacity relief (scaling + pool increase); code fix will take longer to deploy
